# Youth Empowerment Through Environmental Initiatives

![author-avatar](data:image/png;base64,iVBORw0KGgoAAAANSUhEUgAAACAAAAAgAQAAAABbAUdZAAAAAnRSTlMAAHaTzTgAAAAMSURBVAjXY2AY3AAAAKAAAWElfUcAAAAASUVORK5CYII=)
![author-avatar](https://secure.gravatar.com/avatar/1b82d41ee6ad1afe26f50a9b977b9d10?s=32&d=mm&r=g)
![](data:image/png;base64,)
![](https://exploreit.in/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/unnamed.jpg)

In a world facing increasing environmental challenges, empowering youth through environmental initiatives is crucial for fostering sustainable development and creating eco-friendly communities. Young people possess the energy, creativity, and passion needed to drive meaningful environmental change, making their involvement essential for long-term sustainability.

### **The Importance of Youth Empowerment in Environmental Conservation**

Empowering youth in environmental conservation is not only about fostering a sense of responsibility towards the planet but also about harnessing their potential to innovate and lead. Young individuals today are more aware of environmental issues than ever before, thanks to the widespread availability of information and the influence of social media. By engaging youth in environmental initiatives, we can cultivate a generation of environmentally conscious leaders who will continue to champion sustainability throughout their lives.

### **The Role of Youth in Environmental Initiatives**

#### **Why Youth Engagement Matters**

Youth engagement in environmental initiatives matters because young people bring fresh perspectives and innovative ideas to the table. Their willingness to challenge the status quo and experiment with new approaches can lead to groundbreaking solutions for environmental problems. Additionally, involving youth in environmental conservation instills a sense of stewardship and responsibility towards the environment from an early age.

#### **The Power of Youth Activism**

Youth activism has a powerful impact on environmental conservation. Movements like Fridays for Future, initiated by Greta Thunberg, have demonstrated the global influence young people can have when they unite for a cause. These movements not only raise awareness about critical environmental issues but also pressure policymakers and leaders to take decisive action.

#### **Benefits of Youth Participation in Environmental Programs**

Youth participation in environmental programs offers numerous benefits, including the development of leadership skills, enhanced critical thinking, and increased environmental literacy. By taking part in these initiatives, young people gain practical experience in project management, teamwork, and problem-solving. Furthermore, their involvement fosters a deeper connection to nature and a lifelong commitment to environmental stewardship.

### **Types of Environmental Initiatives for Youth**

#### **School-Based Environmental Programs**

Schools play a vital role in promoting environmental awareness among students. School-based environmental programs, such as eco-clubs, recycling initiatives, and school gardens, provide hands-on learning opportunities and encourage students to adopt sustainable practices. These programs can be integrated into the curriculum, making environmental education a core component of students’ learning experiences.

#### **Community Projects and Cleanups**

Community projects and cleanups are effective ways to engage youth in environmental conservation at the local level. Organizing tree planting events, neighborhood cleanups, and community gardens involve young people in tangible actions that have immediate positive impacts on their surroundings. These activities also foster a sense of community and collaboration among participants.

#### **Youth-Led Advocacy Campaigns**

Youth-led advocacy campaigns empower young people to voice their concerns and advocate for environmental policies. Through campaigns, workshops, and public speaking events, youth can influence public opinion and drive legislative changes. These advocacy efforts often involve collaborations with environmental organizations and other stakeholders, amplifying the impact of their messages.

#### **International Environmental Youth Organizations**

International environmental youth organizations provide platforms for young people to connect, share ideas, and collaborate on global environmental issues. Organizations like the United Nations Environment Programme’s (UNEP) Youth and Education Alliance and the Global Youth Biodiversity Network (GYBN) offer opportunities for youth to participate in international conferences, workshops, and projects, broadening their perspectives and enhancing their impact.

### **Implementing Effective Environmental Initiatives**

#### **Designing Youth-Centric Programs**

Designing youth-centric environmental programs requires understanding the interests, motivations, and challenges faced by young people. Programs should be engaging, hands-on, and relevant to their lives. Incorporating interactive elements, such as workshops, field trips, and technology-based activities, can enhance the appeal and effectiveness of these initiatives.

#### **Leveraging Technology and Social Media**

Technology and social media are powerful tools for engaging youth in environmental initiatives. Social media platforms can be used to spread awareness, mobilize participants, and share success stories. Additionally, digital tools and apps can facilitate project management, data collection, and virtual collaboration, making it easier for youth to get involved and stay connected.

#### **Collaboration with Educational Institutions**

Collaboration with educational institutions is crucial for the success of youth environmental programs. Schools, colleges, and universities can provide the necessary resources, infrastructure, and support for implementing these initiatives. Partnerships with educators and administrators ensure that environmental education is integrated into the curriculum and aligned with academic goals.

#### **Government and NGO Support**

Government and NGO support is essential for scaling up youth environmental initiatives. Grants, funding programs, and policy support from governments can provide the financial and regulatory backing needed for these projects. NGOs can offer expertise, resources, and networks to help design and implement effective programs. Collaboration between government bodies, NGOs, and youth organizations can create a robust framework for empowering young people in environmental conservation.

### **Case Studies of Successful Youth Environmental Initiatives**

#### **Global Examples of Youth-Led Environmental Projects**

Around the world, youth-led environmental projects have achieved remarkable success. In India, the “Project GreenHands” initiative, led by the youth wing of the Isha Foundation, has resulted in the planting of millions of trees, significantly improving local ecosystems. Similarly, the “Trash Tag Challenge,” which started as a social media campaign, has inspired thousands of young people worldwide to clean up littered areas, showcasing the power of digital activism.

#### **Local Success Stories**

Local success stories, such as the “Green Team” initiative in Los Angeles, demonstrate the impact of youth-led environmental projects at the community level. This program engages high school students in urban gardening, waste reduction, and environmental education, fostering a sense of responsibility and pride in their local environment. Such initiatives highlight the potential for young people to drive meaningful change within their communities.

### **Challenges and Solutions in Youth Environmental Initiatives**

#### **Overcoming Barriers to Youth Engagement**

Overcoming barriers to youth engagement in environmental initiatives involves addressing factors such as lack of awareness, limited resources, and competing priorities. Providing accessible information, offering incentives, and creating flexible participation options can help attract and retain youth involvement. Engaging parents, educators, and community leaders as allies can also enhance support for these initiatives.

#### **Ensuring Sustained Participation**

Ensuring sustained participation in youth environmental initiatives requires continuous engagement and motivation. Regular feedback, recognition of achievements, and opportunities for personal growth can keep young people invested in these programs. Developing a sense of ownership and responsibility among participants can also foster long-term commitment.

#### **Addressing Resource Limitations**

Resource limitations, such as funding and access to materials, can hinder the implementation of youth environmental initiatives. Creative solutions, such as crowdfunding, partnerships with local businesses, and volunteer support, can help overcome these challenges. Leveraging existing resources, such as school facilities and community spaces, can also maximize impact with minimal investment.

#### **Innovative Solutions and Best Practices**

Innovative solutions and best practices in youth environmental initiatives include integrating environmental education into mainstream curricula, utilizing digital platforms for engagement, and fostering cross-sector collaborations. Sharing successful models and lessons learned can inspire new projects and enhance the effectiveness of existing programs.

### **Future Prospects of Youth in Environmental Conservation**

#### **Innovations in Youth Environmental Programs**

Innovations in youth environmental programs, such as the use of virtual reality for environmental education and the development of youth-friendly apps for tracking environmental impact, are shaping the future of youth engagement. These innovations make environmental initiatives more accessible, engaging, and impactful for young people.

#### **Policy Recommendations for Enhancing Youth Participation**

Policy recommendations for enhancing youth participation in environmental conservation include incorporating environmental education into national curricula, providing funding and support for youth-led projects, and creating platforms for youth voices in policymaking processes. Governments can play a pivotal role in fostering an enabling environment for youth empowerment through supportive policies and programs.

### FAQs

#### **What are the benefits of youth involvement in environmental initiatives?**

Youth involvement in environmental initiatives promotes leadership development, environmental literacy, and a sense of responsibility towards the planet. It also fosters innovation, community engagement, and long-term commitment to sustainability.

#### **How can schools promote environmental awareness among students?**

Schools can promote environmental awareness by integrating environmental education into the curriculum, organizing eco-clubs, conducting workshops and field trips, and involving students in hands-on environmental projects. Collaborations with environmental organizations can also enhance learning experiences.

#### **What are some examples of successful youth-led environmental campaigns?**

Successful youth-led environmental campaigns include the “Fridays for Future” movement, the “Trash Tag Challenge,” and the “Youth Climate Strike.” These campaigns have raised global awareness about environmental issues and mobilized millions of young people to take action.

#### **How can technology be used to engage youth in environmental initiatives?**

Technology can engage youth in environmental initiatives through social media campaigns, educational apps, virtual workshops, and online collaboration platforms. Digital tools can facilitate project management, data collection, and the sharing of success stories, making it easier for youth to participate and stay connected.

#### **What challenges do youth face in participating in environmental projects?**

Challenges faced by youth in participating in environmental projects include lack of awareness, limited resources, competing priorities, and insufficient support from adults. Addressing these challenges requires accessible information, incentives, flexible participation options, and support from parents, educators, and community leaders.

#### **How can communities support youth-led environmental initiatives?**

Communities can support youth-led environmental initiatives by providing resources, funding, and volunteer support. Encouraging collaboration between youth, local businesses, schools, and environmental organizations can also enhance the impact of these projects. Recognizing and celebrating the achievements of young environmentalists fosters a positive and supportive environment.

### Conclusion

Youth empowerment through environmental initiatives is a powerful strategy for fostering sustainable development and eco-friendly communities. By engaging young people in environmental conservation, we can harness their creativity, energy, and passion to drive meaningful change. Effective implementation of youth-centric programs, supported by innovative solutions, policies, and community engagement, can ensure a sustainable and resilient future. As we look ahead, it is essential to continue empowering youth, providing them with the tools and opportunities they need to lead the way towards a greener and more sustainable world.

Update

Youth and the Environment
=========================

![Image 2](https://www.genevaenvironmentnetwork.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/image1170x530cropped-aspect-ratio-2000-1200-300x180.jpg)

Last updated:02 Jun 2025

[Climate](https://www.genevaenvironmentnetwork.org/environment-geneva/key-areas-sdg/key-areas/climate/)|[Human Rights and Environment](https://www.genevaenvironmentnetwork.org/environment-geneva/key-areas-sdg/key-areas/human-rights-environment/)|[Nature](https://www.genevaenvironmentnetwork.org/environment-geneva/key-areas-sdg/key-areas/nature/)

[SDG10](https://www.genevaenvironmentnetwork.org/environment-geneva/key-areas-sdg/sdg/sdg10/)|[SDG11](https://www.genevaenvironmentnetwork.org/environment-geneva/key-areas-sdg/sdg/sdg11/)|[SDG8](https://www.genevaenvironmentnetwork.org/environment-geneva/key-areas-sdg/sdg/sdg8/)|[SDG13](https://www.genevaenvironmentnetwork.org/environment-geneva/key-areas-sdg/sdg/sdg13/)

Young people are central in achieving a more sustainable and healthier planet. Youth have repeatedly demonstrated their willingness and capacity to address environmental and climatic challenges through innovative ideas, demanding policy- and decision-makers for bolder action through advocacy. Meaningful and effective inclusion of young people in environmental action and decision is not only a duty as they represent an important part of the demography and disproportionately bear the consequences of the triple planetary crisis. Doing so ensures better, more resilient, inclusive and sustainable decisions that will have long-term impacts.

**Youth and the Environment**
-----------------------------

Although no universally agreed international definition of the youth age group exists, the United Nations refers to youth as people aged from 15 to 24. Today, there are [1.2 billion](https://www.un.org/en/global-issues/youth) young people aged 15 to 24 years, accounting for 16 percent of the global population. By 2030, the number of youth is projected to have grown by 7 percent, to nearly 1.3 billion. In less than 10 years, when humanity is supposed to deliver the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), it will be people that are currently young and those entering in their youth years that will be bearing the fruits of current environmental and climate decisions and suffer or enjoy their consequences the most.

Young people are not only victims of the environmental and climate crises, they are powerful actors and agents of change that contribute to achieving a more sustainable planet, SDGs, equality and respect of human rights.

> Humanity depends on the boundless energy, ideas and contributions of youth everywhere. Today and every day, let’s support and stand with young people in shaping a just and sustainable world, for people and planet.
> 
>  — [UN Secretary-General António Guterres](https://www.un.org/en/observances/youth-day/messages)

> Young people have helped reshape the global climate debate because they know that they’ll be the ones that could inherit a broken planet
> 
>  — [Inger Andersen, UNEP Executive Director](https://twitter.com/andersen_inger/status/1400709806000857088?lang=en)

In spite of the recognition of youth capacities and potential as a driving motor for a sustainable future, young people often lack access to policy and decision-making spaces and power or struggle to be really listened to and make an impact when included in these processes. To fully unlock youth potential to address the challenges of the triple planetary crisis, they must be granted access to adequate education, skills, and opportunities.

Youth and the Triple Planetary Crisis
-------------------------------------

The triple planetary crisis of biodiversity loss, climate change and pollution affects everyone, but with half of the global population being under the age of 30, the youth is not only the most affected and for the longest part of their lives. It is also less implicated in decision-making processes in response to the triple planetary crisis. Nevertheless, young people from across the world have demonstrated a great sense of awareness, willingness to act and ingenuity in developing solutions to these crises.

### Biodiversity Loss

Young people have demonstrated a highly vested interest in halting biodiversity loss and making conservation more equitable, inclusive and sustainable. Since 2012, the [Global Youth Biodiversity Network](https://www.gybn.org/) contributes and participates to the Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD) processes through advocacy and policy papers on their vision of biodiversity loss governance and instruments and with proposals on how to transform education to discipline young people and future generations on the protection of biodiversity.

### Climate Change

A[global survey published in the Lancet](https://www.thelancet.com/journals/lanplh/article/PIIS2542-5196(21)00278-3/fulltext) found that 59% of young people and children are very or extremely worried and 84% are at least moderately worried. With half of these young people expressing feelings like sadness, anxiety, anger, powerlessness, helplessness, and guilt; climate change negatively affects their daily life and functioning. As a consequence, it is common among young people to lack trust in governmental action over climate change.

Young people have demonstrated a vast array of reactions to this situation. From joining a global partnership of young people asking for more concrete climate actions to their governments through the [Friday’s for the Future Movement](https://fridaysforfuture.org/what-we-do/who-we-are/#:~:text=or%20FFF%2C%20is%20a%20youth,action%20on%20the%20climate%20crisis.) to [climate litigation cases](http://climatecasechart.com/non-us-case-category/youth-children/), young people have raised awareness at local and global levels on the most urgent problem facing humanity. Young people are also promoters of [lifestyle changes and concrete solutions](https://www.undp.org/blog/tapping-power-young-people-climate-action) and advocates for the rights of vulnerable groups, including Indigenous people, who are often excluded from decision-making.

Youth is also present in and vocal about official [climate change negotiations](https://www.un.org/esa/socdev/documents/youth/fact-sheets/youth-climatechange.pdf). Through the organization of parallel events, interventions, and related actions, young people continue advocating for a more enabling environment for children and young people to access and contribute to decision-making processes and for their voices and inhibit equal access. → More information is provided below in the section [Youth @ Climate COPs](https://www.genevaenvironmentnetwork.org/resources/updates/youth-and-the-environment/#:~:text=Global%20Biodiversity%20Framework.-,youth%20%40%20climate%20cops,-Young%20people%E2%80%99s%20participation).

### Pollution

Young people are affected by air, soil, water and waste pollution. Oftentimes, young people end up working in informal waste collection and in agricultural settings where high levels of pesticides are used, risking their health and futures. Young people also represent an important part of consumers, which choices to consume less and better can drastically impact the level of waste pollution generated. Youth is highly engaged in pollution discussions and debates, as demonstrated by the creation of the [Global Youth Coalition on Plastic Pollution (GYCPP)](https://apps1.unep.org/resolutions/uploads/partnershipsforchange_globalyouthcoalition.pdf) on the eve of the [Intergovernmental Negotiating Committee on Plastic Pollution](https://www.genevaenvironmentnetwork.org/resources/updates/towards-plastic-pollution-inc-2/); the active participation on chemicals related processes and negotiations were seen through the Major Group for Children and Youth (MGCY) Chemicals and Waste Platform.

Youth and Human Rights
----------------------

Young people face discrimination and obstacles to the enjoyment of their rights by virtue of their age, limiting their potential. The human rights of youth, therefore,refers to the full enjoyment of fundamental rights and freedoms by young people, including the right to a clean, healthy and sustainable environment. Promoting these rights entails addressing the specific challenges and barriers faced. The Office of the High Commissioner for Human Rights (OHCHR) report on youth and human rights ([A/HRC/39/33](https://www.ohchr.org/EN/Issues/Youth/Pages/ReportYouthHR.aspx)) documents the discrimination and some of the challenges for young people in accessing civil, political, social, economic and cultural rights. Examples include: participation in political institutions and decision-making; access to the world of work and be granted decent and protected working conditions and wages; access to health.

Young people from across the world are also often engaged in environmental protection and climate disobedience. Young [environmental human rights defenders](https://www.genevaenvironmentnetwork.org/resources/updates/environmental-human-rights-defenders/) who promote human rights relating to the environment, including water, air, land, flora and fauna, are highly vulnerable and under attack across the globe. Evidence suggests that as the climate crisis intensifies, violence against EHRDs also increases, whether through assaults, murders, intimidation, harassment, stigmatization, and criminalization. A human-rights-based approach and more protection for young people protecting their environment and future are in constant need of reinforcement.

International Youth Day
-----------------------

In 1999, the United Nations General Assembly endorsed the recommendation by the World Conference of Ministers of Youth (Lisbon, 8-12 August 1998) that 12 August be declared**International Youth Day** ([A/RES/54/120](https://documents-dds-ny.un.org/doc/UNDOC/GEN/N00/246/20/PDF/N0024620.pdf?OpenElement)). Over the last two decades of International Youth Day celebrations, several innovative and timely themes have been explored, including mental health, intergenerational solidarity, safe spaces for youth and civic engagement.

### 2024 Theme | From Clicks to Progress: Youth Digital Pathways for Sustainable Development

Digitalization is transforming our world, offering unprecedented opportunities to accelerate sustainable development. Digital technologies such as mobile devices, services, and artificial intelligence are instrumental in advancing the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). Data generated from digital interactions supports evidence-based decision-making. With profound impact across economic, social and environmental dimensions,[digital technologies and data contribute to at least 70 per cent of the 169 SDG targets](https://www.undp.org/sites/g/files/zskgke326/files/2023-09/SDG%20Digital%20Acceleration%20Agenda_2.pdf)while potentially[reducing the cost of achieving these goals by up to USD 55 trillion](https://sdg.iisd.org/news/force-for-good-report-explores-role-of-technology-in-helping-reach-sdgs/).

Young people are leading the charge in digital adoption and innovation, with[three-quarters of those aged 15 to 24 using the internet in 2022](https://www.itu.int/itu-d/reports/statistics/2022/11/24/ff22-youth-internet-use/), a rate higher than other age groups. However, disparities persist, particularly in low-income countries and among young women, who often have less access to the internet and digital skills compared to their male counterparts. While there is an urgent need to enhance digital inclusion, youth are largely recognized as “digital natives,” using technology to drive change and create solutions. As the 2030 deadline for the SDGs approaches, the role of young people in digital innovation is essential for addressing global issues.

By celebrating the digital contributions of youth, we can inspire further innovation and collaboration towards achieving sustainable development.

For the observance of International Youth Day 2024, UN DESA collaborates with key partners, including International Telecommunication Union (ITU), UN-Habitat, and UN Internet Governance Forum (IGF). Together, these partnerships will highlight the transformative potential of youth-driven digital solutions in advancing sustainable development.

![Image 3](https://www.genevaenvironmentnetwork.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/08/INT_Youth_Day_2024_-_logo_E-w-UN.png)

[Official Website](https://social.desa.un.org/Issues/Youth/events/international-youth-day-2024?_gl=1*40jing*_ga*NDgzMTY4NzgzLjE2ODAyNDU1MjU.*_ga_TK9BQL5X7Z*MTcyMzE4Njg0OS40MTkuMS4xNzIzMTg2ODYzLjAuMC4w)

### Events

![Image 4](https://www.genevaenvironmentnetwork.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/08/ILO-aspect-ratio-1000-600-300x180.jpg)

### Previous Observations

2023 | Green Skills for Youth: Towards a Sustainable World

The shift towards an environmentally sustainable and climate-friendly world is critical not only for responding to the global climate crisis but also for achieving the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). A successful transition towards a greener world will depend on the development of green skills in the population. Green skills are “knowledge, abilities, values and attitudes needed to live in, develop and support a sustainable and resource-efficient society”.

These include technical knowledge and skills that enable the effective use of green technologies and processes in occupational settings, as well as transversal skills that draw on a range of knowledge, values and attitudes to facilitate environmentally sustainable decisions in work and in life. Due to their interdisciplinary nature, the essence of green skills is sometimes expressed, partly if not wholly, through other associated terms such as “skills for the future” and “skills for green jobs”. While green skills are relevant for people of all ages, they have heightened importance for younger people, who can contribute to the green transition for a longer period of time.

Despite the growing recognition ofthe relevance of green skills, the available evidence suggests a shortage of such skills among young people. The skills gap represents an obstacle for young people to participate in a world of work that is shifting towards a green economy. If current trends persist, by 2030 more than 60 percent of young people may lack the skills required to thrive in the green economy. The 2023 edition of International Youth Day will focus on green skills for youth, highlighting initiatives at the global and national levels, discussing relevant opportunities and challenges, and offering forward looking policy guidance.

For the official commemoration of International Youth Day 2023, DESA will organize a [global webinar](https://social.desa.un.org/issues/youth/events/international-youth-day-2023) in collaboration with the UN Global Initiative on Decent Jobs for Youth and Generation Unlimited on 12 August 2023.

![Image 9](https://www.genevaenvironmentnetwork.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/Screen-Shot-2023-07-27-at-4.59.30-PM-e1691576017262.png)

#### Geneva Celebrations of International Youth Day

[International Youth Day 2023 | Green Skills for Youth: Towards a Sustainable World](https://www.genevaenvironmentnetwork.org/events/youth-day23-empowering-youth-ecopreneurs/) | 15 August 2023 | International Trade Center, World Intellectual Property Organization & Sidley

Inclusion of Youth in UN Processes
----------------------------------

Since the UN launched the International Youth Year in 1985, the inclusion and participation of young people in UN processes have progressively increased and improved. At the Earth Summit held in Rio de Janeiro in 1992, parties r ecognized that achieving sustainable development requires the active participation of all sectors of society and all types of people. As a result, [Agenda 21](https://sustainabledevelopment.un.org/outcomedocuments/agenda21), formalized nine sectors and rightsholder groups of society as the main channels through which broad participation would be facilitated in UN activities related to sustainable development. These are most commonly referred to as “[Major Groups and other Stakeholders](https://sustainabledevelopment.un.org/aboutmajorgroups.html)”.

In 1995, on the tenth anniversary of International Youth Year, the UN strengthened its commitment to young people by adopting an international strategy — the [World Programme of Action for Youth to the Year 2000 and Beyond](http://www.un.org/Docs/journal/asp/ws.asp?m=A/RES/50/81).

Young people’s interventions in environmental-related processes and negotiations have become historic for their framing of adults’ lack of action and their failure to preserve the planet for current and future generations, leaving such responsibility to young people themselves.

**15-year-old activist Greta Thunberg speaks at the UN Climate Change COP24 Conference in Katowice in 2018.**

### Youth @ UNEA

Children and youth are among the Major Groups accredited to UNEP and the UN Environment Assembly (UNEA). Currently, [51 organizations](https://www.unep.org/civil-society-engagement/accreditation/list-accredited-organizations?_ga=2.83745812.347257166.1691390375-100556824.1680252293) from across the world can get directly involved with UNEP. [Children and Youth Major Group to UNEP (MGCY)](https://www.youthenvironment.org/unea) is the official participation mechanism for children and youth and children-/ and youth-led organizations in UNEA. The group creates working groups on leading topics of the UNEA and consults children and youth globally in order to assess their opinion on the topics negotiated. These positions are then transformed into position papers which are submitted to the member states as well as oral interventions during the negotiations. UNEP MGCY prepares negotiations internally and sends a group of representatives to UNEA and most CPR and CPR Subcommittee meetings in order to represent children and youth voices.

In preparations to the Sixth Session of the UN Environment Assembly (UNEA-6), UNEP MGCY provided [inputs to the ministerial declaration](https://wedocs.unep.org/bitstream/handle/20.500.11822/42911/ZeroDraftMD_CYMG.pdf) to be adopted at the session.

### Youth Environment Assembly

Since 2020, the Children and Youth Major Group of UNEP organizes the Youth Environment Assembly. Taking place in the run-up and/or as part of the UNEA, t he Assembly comprises sessions on a variety of environmental topics, interactive segments, regional and thematic breakouts, dedicated plenary sessions and high-level segments featuring dialogues between environment ministers and youth. As outcome of the [Global Youth Environment Assembly](https://www.cymgenv.net/gyea)[2024](https://www.cymgenv.net/gyea), the CYMG released the [Global Youth Declaration on Environment 2024](https://www.cymgenv.net/gyd).

### Youth @ Stockholm+50

The [Stockholm+5o international meeting](https://www.stockholm50.global/news-and-stories/youth-firmly-spotlight-stockholm50) gathered over 300 young people from all over the world and saw the participation of more than 700 in the Youth Task Force to Stockholm+50, which worked on the preparations to the meeting. After meeting with the UN Secretary-General and UNEP Executive Director Inger Andersen, the Youth Task Force agreed to the [Global Youth Policy Paper](https://www.youthstockholm50.global/global-youth-policy-paper). It demands governments to focus on actions for a healthy planet, COVID-19 recovery, the environmental dimension of sustainable development, and ensuring inclusive decision-making. An event organized by the International Labour Organization (ILO), UNEP, and the UN International Children’s Emergency Fund (UNICEF), focused on boosting green jobs and green, employable skills in key countries and high-impact sectors announced the creation of the [Green Jobs for Youth Pact](https://www.climateaction4jobs.org/youth/), later established at the Climate Change COP27 in November 2022.

### Youth @ Conference on Biological Diversity

The [Secretariat of the Conference on Biological Diversity (CBD)](https://www.cbd.int/youth/) invites youth and children in participating to the presentation, protection and restoration of biodiversity. Even prior to the establishment of the Global Youth Biodiversity Network in 2012, [young people have participated and contributed](https://www.gybn.org/policy) to CBD’s conferences of the parties. [COP11 Decision XI/8](https://www.cbd.int/decisions/cop/11/8) formally recognized the importance of youth participation in the decision-making process at all levels. Youth actively participated in the first and second part of the UN Biodiversity Conference (2021-2022).

 Among other activities, young delegates led a Youth Summit, a Youth Day, a youth hub in Place Quebec, side-events, exhibitions, marches and caucuses to coordinate young’s people views on the CBD negotiations including on the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework.

### Youth @ Climate COPs

Young people’s participation in Climate Change Conferences of the Parties started to become more institutionalized [since COP10 in Buenos Aires](https://unfccc.int/sites/default/files/youth_participation_in_the_unfccc_negotiations.pdf) when young people came together as an “organized caucus” for the first time drafting a declaration that called for “the recognition of a Youth Constituency Group within the COP in order to assure youth participation, in line with other groups such as businesses, indigenous people and environmental organizations.” Since COP11, young people constituencies (officially [YOUNGO](http://www.youngo.uno/)) have organized [Conferences of Youth (COY)](https://unfccc.int/topics/education-and-youth/youngo/coy) ahead of COPs. Built on four major components of policy document; capacity building; skill-building workshops; and cultural e

## Mobilizing the Next Generation of Leaders To Shape the Future of Sustainable Development.

## YECI

### Youth for Environmental Conservation Initiative

![](https://youthforenvironmentalconservation.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/EXTRA3-1.jpg)

### ABOUT US

## Youth for Environmental Conservation Initiative (YECI)

Formely ***Friends of Nature Network*** is a youth-led, gender-inclusive organization championing biodiversity conservation and climate resilience across Africa’s rural communities. Focusing on inclusivity, YECI bridges generational and social divides to empower young people and marginalized populations as key agents of environmental stewardship.

By addressing the socio-economic factors driving environmental degradation, we foster intergenerational collaboration and nurture sustainable development initiatives.

YECI's mission is rooted in building resilient communities through environmental literacy, citizen science, and policy advocacy. The organization prioritizes participatory approaches, engaging local voices to identify challenges, craft grassroots solutions, and achieve tangible outcomes. By blending youth-led innovation with indigenous knowledge, YECI ensures that its interventions are both effective and socially, environmentally and culturally aligned.

## Through capacity-building programs, peer mentorship, and grant support,YECI equips emerging leaders with the tools needed to address pressing environmental challenges

## We Foster A Sense of Global Citizenship And Civil Responsibility Through Environmental Literacy

We equip, engage, and empower young people to become active stewards of the environment. Explore what we stand for and the values that drive everything we do.

### OUR IMPACT STORIES

## Hear From Youths Who Have Benefitted From Our Programs

![](https://youthforenvironmentalconservation.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/TANATSWA-MAPFUMO.-1.jpg)

I became a Focal Point for the Youth for Environmental Conservation Initiative in February 2024, stepping into the climate action space with very little to no experience but a deep curiosity. This role became the turning point in my career, shaping my understanding of environmental advocacy and action.
Thanks to the incredible team, I gained the confidence and knowledge to apply for prestigious opportunities, including grants, which have helped me transition from a climate enthusiast to a dedicated advocate for sustainability.
YECI is not just about nurturing changemakers for a better environment, it is shaping socially responsible individuals who will, in turn, inspire others to take action.

![](https://youthforenvironmentalconservation.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/DAMOLA.jpg)

From starting out as a Campus Focal Point to becoming a Program Coordinator of this incredible community, my journey has been filled with growth, hard work, and perseverance.
I joined YECI in 2024 and quickly immersed myself in leading key initiatives, including Cleanup Week and several impactful outreach programs. These experiences didn’t just shape events — they shaped me.
Through YECI, I’ve built strong leadership and communication skills that I will carry with me for life. I’m deeply grateful for the opportunity to serve and grow within a community so committed to meaningful change.

![](https://youthforenvironmentalconservation.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/VALEX.jpg)

As a Campus Focal Point at the Institut National Polytechnique Félix Houphouët-Boigny, my commitment to environmental sustainability has deepened.
Through meaningful initiatives and community-driven actions, I have played a key role in fostering ecological awareness and inspiring positive change. These efforts have not only strengthened my advocacy but have also empowered my community to embrace more sustainable practices.

![](https://youthforenvironmentalconservation.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/mociruku-joyce.jpg)

As a young African changemaker, joining YECI as a mentee gave me the validation I needed. I did not only get access to life-changing skills to transform my career as an environmentalist and network with climate professionals.
I found a lifelong community to support my work

enriched_points.expanded_content: Engaging young people in environmental initiatives cultivates leaders who drive sustainable change, fostering creativity and innovation essential for addressing pressing ecological issues.
By equipping youth with knowledge and skills, we create a resilient generation committed to preserving natural resources and advocating for policies that ensure environmental sustainability.
